q: CASE STUDY — FinSecure bank requires: no standing admin access, MFA on activation, quarterly access certification, and full audit of privileged actions. Which THREE features do you combine?
o: PIM eligible assignments with MFA on activation
PIM access reviews
Entra audit logs / Log Analytics export
Resource locks
Azure Policy deny effect
e: PIM eligibility+MFA kills standing access, access reviews give certification, audit logs provide the trail. Locks and Policy govern resources, not privileged identity.

q: CASE STUDY — ShopSphere must keep EU customer PII in the EU while serving a global app, with regional failover that stays EU-compliant. Which TWO design choices are correct?
o: Azure SQL in West Europe with failover group to North Europe
Cosmos DB with write regions in East US and West Europe for PII
Store PII pseudonymized references only in non-EU regions
Enable RA-GRS from West Europe to East US for the PII database
e: WEU→NEU keeps both replicas in the EU; non-EU regions hold only pseudonymized references. Replicating PII to US regions (options B/D) violates residency.

q: Which THREE are required for a working private endpoint to Azure SQL from on-premises via ExpressRoute?
o: Private endpoint in a VNet subnet
Private DNS zone privatelink.database.windows.net with correct records reachable from on-prem resolvers
ER private peering routing to the VNet
Service endpoint on the gateway subnet
Public firewall allow rule for the on-prem NAT IP
e: PE + DNS resolution to the private IP + private-peering routing are the triad. Service endpoints don't work from on-prem; public firewall rules defeat the purpose.

q: An API validates incoming JWTs. Which FOUR checks are mandatory before trusting the token?
o: Signature against the issuer's published keys (JWKS)
Issuer (iss) claim
Audience (aud) claim
Expiry (exp/nbf)
Token length > 500 characters
e: Signature, issuer, audience, and time validity are the core validation set; token length is meaningless.

q: Which TWO protect backups against ransomware actors with stolen admin credentials?
o: Immutable vault
Multi-user authorization for destructive operations
GRS redundancy
Longer retention
e: Immutability blocks tampering; MUA requires a second identity to approve destructive changes. GRS/retention don't stop malicious deletion attempts.

q: Which TWO Cosmos DB choices best fit an unpredictable, low-average, spiky workload with occasional bursts?
o: Serverless capacity mode
Autoscale provisioned throughput
Standard (manual) provisioned at peak
Strong consistency to smooth load
e: Serverless (low/spiky) or autoscale (variable but sustained) both avoid paying peak 24/7. Manual-at-peak wastes money; consistency isn't a capacity lever.

q: Which THREE belong in an enterprise APIM security baseline for a public API?
o: validate-jwt (or validate-azure-ad-token) at the gateway
WAF (Front Door/App GW) in front of APIM
Managed identity or mTLS from APIM to backends
Rely on subscription keys as sole authentication
Expose backends publicly for redundancy
e: Token validation + WAF edge + secured backend channel. Subscription keys alone aren't authentication; public backends bypass the gateway.

q: Arrange the CAF migration journey in the correct order:
o: Strategy — define business justification
Plan — inventory and skill planning
Ready — build landing zones
Adopt — migrate and innovate
Govern & Manage — ongoing operations
e: CAF: Strategy → Plan → Ready → Adopt, with Govern and Manage as continuing disciplines.

q: Arrange the regional failover runbook for the ForgeWorks MES (SQL MI failover group + ASR + Traffic Manager):
o: Confirm regional outage via Service Health and monitoring
Fail over the SQL MI auto-failover group to the secondary
Execute the ASR recovery plan to boot app VMs in order
Verify app health probes in the secondary region
Switch Traffic Manager/Front Door priority to the secondary
e: Verify the disaster first, bring data tier up, then compute, validate, then shift traffic — shifting traffic before the stack is healthy causes a second outage.

q: Arrange the OAuth 2.0 authorization code + PKCE flow:
o: Client generates code_verifier and code_challenge
User authenticates at Entra ID authorize endpoint (challenge sent)
Entra returns an authorization code to the redirect URI
Client exchanges code + code_verifier at the token endpoint
Client calls the API with the access token
e: PKCE binds the code exchange to the verifier, preventing code interception attacks.

q: Arrange the packet path for an internet user reaching an internal-VNet APIM-fronted API:
o: User resolves the app domain to Front Door anycast edge
Front Door applies WAF rules and routes to origin via Private Link
Internal APIM gateway validates JWT and applies policies
APIM forwards to the Container Apps internal ingress
Container app calls the data tier over private endpoints
e: Edge → WAF → gateway policies → private backend → private data — every hop private after the edge.

q: Arrange an online (minimal-downtime) SQL Server → SQL MI migration:
o: Run Data Migration Assistant compatibility assessment
Provision SQL Managed Instance and networking
Start DMS online migration (continuous sync)
Validate data and performance on the target
Cutover: stop writes, complete sync, repoint connection strings
e: Assess → provision → sync → validate → brief cutover window. Offline mode would move the downtime to the full copy phase.

q: Arrange the GitOps deployment flow for RetailRocket's AKS platform:
o: Developer merges code PR; GitHub Actions builds and scans the image
Pipeline pushes the signed image to ACR
Pipeline opens a PR updating the image tag in the GitOps config repo
Flux detects the merged config change and reconciles the cluster
Canary weights shift traffic; burn-rate alerts gate promotion or rollback
e: CI produces artifacts; CD is a Git merge that Flux pulls — pipelines never hold cluster credentials.

q: CASE STUDY — 'Minimize administrative effort' + 'apps must not change connection strings after regional DB failover' + 'automatic failover'. Recommend:
o: Active geo-replication with manual DNS updates
Auto-failover groups with grace period
Geo-restore runbook
Cross-region read replica promotion
e: Failover groups: automatic policy + listener endpoints = zero app changes, minimal admin effort — every qualifier satisfied.

q: CASE STUDY — A workload needs 99.99% compute SLA in ONE region at the LOWEST cost. Recommend:
o: Two VMs in an availability set
VMs spread across availability zones behind a Standard LB
Single VM with premium SSD
Multi-region active-active
e: Zone spread hits 99.99%; availability sets top out at 99.95%; multi-region exceeds requirement (and budget).

q: CASE STUDY — CloudNest tenants must view only their own spoke's logs; the SOC needs estate-wide correlation. Cheapest compliant design:
o: 50 workspaces + 1 SOC workspace with cross-workspace queries
One central workspace: resource-context RBAC for tenants, Sentinel for SOC
Export each tenant's logs to their own storage account
Per-tenant Sentinel instances
e: Central workspace + resource-context access satisfies isolation and correlation with one ingestion pipeline; 50 workspaces multiplies cost and ops.

q: CALCULATE — A read-heavy Cosmos container needs 8,000 RU/s at peak (4 h/day) and 800 RU/s otherwise. Which throughput model is cheapest?
o: Standard provisioned 8,000 RU/s
Autoscale with max 8,000 RU/s (scales 800–8,000)
Serverless
Two containers
e: Autoscale bills per-hour on actual scale between 10%–100% of max — ideal for daily peaks. Standard pays 8,000 all day; serverless gets expensive at sustained high RU consumption.

q: CALCULATE — App Gateway (99.95%) → 2 zone-redundant web VMs (99.99%) → SQL zone-redundant (99.99%). Approximate composite SLA:
e: Serial chain: 0.9995 × 0.9999 × 0.9999 ≈ 0.9993 → 99.93%. The weakest serial link dominates.

q: TROUBLESHOOT — An ACA app with minReplicas=0 shows 8-second first-request latency each morning. Cheapest fix preserving off-hours savings:
o: minReplicas=1 always
Scheduled scale rule (cron) warming one replica during business hours
Move to AKS
Bigger CPU allocation
e: A cron scale rule warms replicas only when users are active — cold starts eliminated during hours, scale-to-zero kept overnight.

q: TROUBLESHOOT — Orders are processed twice occasionally under load. Consumers take ~90 s per message; lock duration is 60 s. Best fix:
o: Switch to receive-and-delete
Renew locks (or raise lock duration) AND make handlers idempotent
Add more consumers
Disable dead-lettering
e: Lock expiry mid-processing causes redelivery. Fix the lock lifetime and keep idempotency — at-least-once delivery makes duplicates always possible.
